title: Equity vs Cash

content.question: Are you chasing cash when you should be stacking equity?
sop: List your income sources and mark each as 'cash' (spent, gone) or 'equity' (owned, compounds).
Calculate what share of this month's income went into equity-building moves: investments, owned property, business ownership stakes, IP.
Tally your total Assets and total Liabilities to get a real net worth number, not a guess (see calculation, page 5).
Pick one recurring cash expense to redirect into an equity-building move next month.
Review quarterly, equity that isn't growing is just cash with extra steps.
questions: Are you chasing cash when you should be stacking equity?
If you stopped working today, what would still be worth something in five years, and what would evaporate?
What percentage of this month's income went toward something you'll still own later, versus something you'll just spend?

definitions.def: Ownership stake in an asset or business that grows in value over time, unlike cash income which is spent and gone.
Intellectual property; things you own that aren't physical, like a brand, content, trademarks, patents, or software, that can keep producing value without ongoing labor.

content.equation_example: Assets ($ you own: cash, investments, property, business equity) minus Liabilities ($ you owe) equals Net Worth. Assets $150,000 minus Liabilities $40,000 equals $110,000. Rising net worth means you're building wealth that compounds; flat or falling means income is being consumed as fast as it arrives.
content.closing_statement: Own things that grow, not just money that's spent.

caption:
Principle Nº 172: Equity vs Cash

Ownership compounds; labor doesn't.

Ask yourself: Are you chasing cash when you should be stacking equity?
